narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in this field, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admissions model looks at patterns from past students—like their early and later school grades, LSAT scores, undergraduate GPAs, family income levels, and whether they plan to study full-time or part-time—to predict the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try. For you, it calculated a 74.5% probability that you might not pass the bar initially, which is high enough that the school uses it as a reason to deny admission to minimize risks for everyone involved. The biggest factor pushing toward this "fail" prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom group compared to most students who tend to rank much higher on average. Your Year 1 decile was also low, adding to the model's concern since stronger early performance often signals better bar outcomes. Planning to attend part-time, which is uncommon (only about 7% of students do), nudged the prediction further toward failure, as full-time students historically fare better. On a brighter note, your LSAT score, undergraduate GPA, and middle family income bracket each worked in your favor, pulling the prediction slightly toward passing the bar—though not enough to outweigh the top concerns. Compared to typical applicants, who average higher deciles around the middle of their classes, much stronger LSATs near 37, and GPAs over 3.2, your profile stood out in ways that raised red flags for the model. This isn't a judgment on your potential or the barriers you've overcome as a Black woman in academia—it's just the system's data-driven snapshot. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, seeking ways to improve your academic deciles through additional coursework or programs, and exploring full-time options if possible, as these could shift future predictions in your favor. You might also appeal with a personal statement highlighting your unique strengths and resilience, or apply to schools with more holistic reviews. Keep pushing—you have every right to claim your place in law, and with targeted improvements, you'll be better positioned next time.
